author: @curtisnixon5313
text: My favourite steam locomotives are the 9F 92220 Evening Star, the last to be built by British Rail in 1960, 10 drive wheels, which is at York; and A1 60163 Tornado, started in 1994, completion date of 2008, and the first new locomotive built in the UK since Evening Star, featured on the Top Gear race from London to Edinburgh! It's been officially recorded as doing over 100mph!

author: @SheplerStudios
text: Love steam engines (Stirlings as well). My great-great-grandfather, his son and his grandson (my grandfather) all operated stationary steam engines, the last being a Corliss model with a chain-grate stoker at a local university’s cogeneration power plant. Tunnels ran from the power plant to the campus building distributing electricity and steam heat and return condensation. My Dad showed me the building where his Dad and grandfather had run the steam engine, which was scraped during a WWII defense department metal drive.

author: @martinjandijkstra3205

author: @billbaggins
text:
You win Rob 🍺 for reasons ↓
Obviously for your passion, honesty, knowledge, generosity and all the other things that tell me we would enjoy a few beers and interesting thoughts, but mostly cos my brain is starting to work again.
I guess there was something ....magnetic😏 about the tornado/ Magnus device that started me thinking deeper. Love the Heath Robinson approach but critical thinking tells me that it was a pretty dodgy setup and needs better controls.. A fair point.Then I thort, how can I make something similar but more amenable to accurate measurements. That got complicated very quickly.
Then I thort some more, the minimal extra energy and complexity of adding a Magnus device seems barely worth the effort in a windy setting and considering the advances in wind turbines. I remember the Flettner drive ships being exciting but failing for various reasons.

What about a region with low wind but ample sun?  Same idea but bigger. 3,  6,  12 ? Magnus cylinders on a base round a central axis. Solar panels on top of the cyliders could power motor/ generators on those 3 axes to generate Magnus and surely the turbulence behind one cylinder would provide friction for the next one etc and accumulate potential. A squirrel cage type fan atop the cylinder in conjunction with the solar and the motor/generators could be used when the odd storm comes. May need to combine with a large flywheel system and need a "kickstart" . Micro hydro, bore well pressure, thermal activity could also be used.
I dunno, I'm not a physicist and certainly can't do the math involved but the idea of harnessing some of the weaker forces is intriguing.

Quite happy to be shot down in flames but interested in any thoughts 👍
Cheers and thanks Rob 🍺

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: you know me mate - I never shoot things down - to me the fact you are thinking is what matters most. Magnus generators are one of those areas of interest in the research and a lot of work is going on - more than folks think - there are both HAWT and VAWT arrangements with devices claimed to work in the 2m/s wind speed range - you are right what I built was a bit 'ropey' but that's ok as it really is just to introduce the idea and get folks thinking - looks like it worked at that objective lol have a read - you will be amazed to see what folks are actually up to - cheers

replies.author: @billbaggins
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering 😊 Cheers mate 🍺 I tried to align the magnet with Applied Science latest vid to help explain how unrelated things lead to new discoveries, maybe that will open eyes

author: @RealMechDruid
text: Off topic: Nano(Nuclear) Diamond Battery.  Using C14 carbon from irradiated control rods which are powdered and pressed into a diamond.  Big issue for me is energy density and efficiency.  Only 4% of material will be radioactive.  Promise of the technology is a battery life in excess of 1,000 years.

author: @NorthernKitty
text: I enjoyed this visit very much!!  It's nice to think we share a love of "steampunk" with our ancestors.  😋   You mentioned the impressive compact size of the piston as compared to the steam engine... that's made all the more impressive considering those engines consisted mostly of cast iron!!  They probably weighed some 100,000 kilograms or more.  Not only technological marvels of their day, but they continue to be a marvel in the modern age.  We often like to think ourselves as smarter than our ancestors, but we forget they didn't have the same luxuries of information, tools and materials we have today.  In many ways, I think they were more ingenious and inventive.  For instance, Babbage's analytical engine design just blows me away.  I think I very much would have enjoyed being around in the 1800's.  I envy Ada Lovelace!!  😄

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: absolutely - I sometimes think I was born out of time - what would we have been back then lol

author: @JSabh
text: Steam powered my US NAVY journey, ok nuclear power did but still, the nuclear reactors are really just steam generators lol. I do wonder why people demonize gasoline and diesel though, oil is absolutely renewable. If grown rather than pumped up from underground, the CO2 emitted by the use of the fuel produced will have been absorbed previously by the plant to reach the point of usefulness. "Fossil fuels" is the most ignorant term out there, what fossil? The same can be derived from living plant life and animal life. It is renewable and green, I am all for alternative sources but, only because they offer a redundancy. BTW, Great " Coffee with Rob" kinda morning. Cheers mate.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: spot on mate nuclear really is just upgraded steam

author: @sofa-lofa4241
text:
Looks like a great day out Rob,
I went to the national railway museum in York a few years back, also well worth a visit,
Another great place is Crossness pumping station near Woolwich, it is a cathedral to victorian ingenuity, the beam engines there are amazing, make sure you go on a 'steam' day, when they fire them up and run them, 

The Bluebell railway isn't far from you Rob, have you been yet?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I have never been to crossness mate - I've seen it but there is nothing like being there - I must go

replies.author: @sofa-lofa4241
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering yes, you really should mate, the 50 ton beams and the ornate cast iron work would be right up your street,
The centre of the octagonal control floor looks like a steampunk tardis... bonus! 
You don't just watch them work, you can feel it right through your body, amazing sounds too, 

In the sheds next to the engine house you will find the volunteer engineers that keep everything ticking like a watch, well worth having a chat to these guys, they know a thing or two about engines and are very friendly and approachable, 

The next steaming day is on Sunday 25th October but will need booking as early as possible as there is a morning and afternoon session but numbers restricted to 150 people per session, I'm sure they will sell the tickets very quickly, it is a great day out for a tenner

replies.author: @PhilWaud
replies.text: The whole industrial revolution era was amazing - if you look at maps from any town, there was an explosion in development around that time, small village before, then a huge town with extravagant, lavish buildings, proper roads, electric street lights, running water, proper sanitation (eventually), canals, railroad - its dazzling to think that it was all down so quickly.

replies.author: @pulesjet
replies.text: @PhilWaud I Live in New Mexico down by the dotted line of mexico.  Every town down here is 50-60 miles distance.   That's how far the rail could go before needing water.  Even here in the Dry S.W. Desert they found water.   How many people Died for lack of water until then ?  All happened over the 1800-1860 time frame.   The locals weren't so pleased with the encroachment.  Most all towns in the North of Mexico was established the same way.
